data.title: Season’s feastings
data.description:
Parkroyal Collection Marina Bay, Singapore

Have a lush, botanical Christmas at Parkroyal Collection Marina Bay, Singapore, where yuletide cheer comes in the form of sumptuous buffets, delectable afternoon teas and fabulous festive bundles. 

At Peppermint, festive feasts (from S$72 per adult) spotlight farm-to-table herbs, responsibly sourced seafood and succulent applewood-grilled meats. Highlights include Boston lobster, Russian snow crab, slow-cooked turkey with Indian spices, lavender-infused chicken ham, hay-smoked salmon and juicy applewood wagyu beef belly.

On Christmas Eve and Christmas Day, indulgence peaks with beef wellington, whole duck morel and pistachio, and baked lobster yuzu thermidor. 

For breezy afternoon catch-ups, Portman’s Bar presents an exquisite afternoon tea spread (from S$68 per pax) that is sure to delight. Nibble on tasty bites such as crab salad cones with mango and shiny bush cress and braised Black Angus beef in yorkshire pudding, and fun desserts such as green tea cheesecakes shaped as Christmas trees and chocolate chestnut reindeer.

Add on free-flow bubbles, wine or beer (from S$118 per pax) for a fancy touch, or kick back with seasonal and zero-proof cocktails for a merry sparkle. 

Prefer celebrations in the comfort of home? The hotel’s festive family bundle for four to six (S$398) makes dinner a piece of cake with a rich spread including Christmas ambrosia salad, roasted French poulet, black truffle and wild mushroom lasagna, and chocolate banana log cake.

You can also put together your own feast with traditional roasted tom turkey (S$248), roasted turducken (S$348) and a dazzling array of log cakes in flavours such as the best-selling chocolate banana (S$89), hazelnut apricot (S$89) and cheesy citrus marron (S$85).

Prices are before service charge and tax. For more information, visit: https://bit.ly/4pyKPwQ

Pan Pacific Singapore

The festive season bursts into life with artistic flair at Pan Pacific Singapore, as holiday celebrations are brushed with culinary craftsmanship, family fun and memorable yuletide indulgence. 

Delight friends, family and business associates with a lavish festive hamper, from the opulent Pacific Festive Hamper (S$499 net) featuring Taittinger champagne and house-made stollen, to the Delicatessen Deluxe Hamper (S$330 net). 

Holiday feasting starts at home with a full suite of festive takeaways, starring master butcher Haiko Mayer’s gourmet roasts such as pork roulade (S$227 net) and braised lamb shank (S$172 net). 

Seafood lovers will revel in the oven-baked whole red snapper (S$249 net) and cheese-baked lobster thermidor (S$249 net). 

Relaxing home celebrations are easy with curated bundles. Pick from the premium festive bundle (S$576 net) with roast turkey, lobster thermidor and chocolate log cake, and the traditional festive bundle (S$358 net) containing oven-baked red snapper and rum-infused fruit cake. Sweet delights include pure chocolate log cake (S$88 net), white peach tea log cake (S$88 net) and handcrafted pralines (from S$22 net for six pieces).

Across the hotel, festive dining unfolds in vibrant style. Toast to happiness with holiday cocktails such as mulled wine (S$20) and classic eggnog (S$20) at Plume. Gather with loved ones at Edge over buffets packed with seafood, roasts and Christmas favourites (from S$88 per adult for weekday lunch). Hai Tien Lo offers a joyous six-course Cantonese celebratory menu (S$208 per pax), while Keyaki enchants with exquisite winter kaiseki menus (from S$250 per pax). 

Over at Pacific Emporium, the festive afternoon tea (from S$68 per pax) and set menus (from S$35) bring heartwarming flavours to relaxed gatherings.

Prices are before service charge and tax, unless stated otherwise. For more information, visit: https://panpacificsingapore.oddle.me/en_SG

Orchard Hotel

Orchard Hotel Singapore sets the scene for a luxurious Christmas, where vibrant purple tones and festive sparkle add glamour to gourmet indulgence.

Begin your festive shopping at The Merry Haus, a cheery red-and-white space offering artisanal goodies and premium hampers (S$158 to S$398). Stealing the spotlight are two new takeaway creations: the decadent A5 Japanese Miyazaki wagyu beef rib-eye (S$488) served with ponzu and Himalayan salt, and the spectacular roasted lechon (S$999, 13 to 15 kg), complete with shatteringly crisp skin, tender meat and traditional Filipino Mang Tomas sauce.

The festive line-up continues with luxurious centrepieces perfect for any party table, including the manuka honey and Macallan whisky baked bone-in gammon ham (S$368), traditional Norwegian salmon trout wellington (S$260) and Hua Ting’s signature roasted Irish duck (S$168).

For sweets, pastry chef Nicole presents five elegant festive cakes (S$92 each) in playful flavours such as crunchy hazelnut praline in fresh Chantilly cream, raspberry pistachio and matcha fraise latte.

At The Orchard Cafe, lavish festive buffets (S$94 to S$150 per pax) promise generous spreads of seafood, live stations, Asian signatures and decadent desserts. Highlights include French-style butter and black truffle-infused roasted whole turkey, Spanish lechon, wok-fried chilli and pepper crab, pappardelle pasta lamb ragout in a parmesan wheel, and the hotel’s beloved durian pengat.

For a refined alternative, Hua Ting offers Cantonese festive set menus (from S$148 per pax) featuring exquisite dishes such as sauteed bird’s nest, braised whole eight-head abalone and smoked crispy roasted duck. 

Those seeking a relaxed celebration can savour Bar Intermezzo’s festive high tea (S$40 per pax), which features a charming medley of yuletide savouries and sweets.

Prices are before service charge and tax. For more information, visit: https://bit.ly/OHS-Festive-Treats

Carlton Hotel Singapore

Carlton Hotel Singapore dials up the holiday cheer with a joyful line-up of festive treats, hearty roasts, and merry experiences for the whole family. 

The hotel’s Christmas takeaways make home celebrations effortlessly magical, starring centrepieces such as roasted turkey (S$188), slow-roasted black pepper beef (S$268), and a show-stopping honey-glazed bone-in gammon ham (S$288) with pineapple and raisin sauce.

For something crisp and comforting, the German pork knuckle with barbecue sauce (S$138 for two 1.2 kg pieces) promises crackling perfection, while the Christmas Party Bundle (S$338) of roasted turkey, honey-glazed boneless ham and a Carlton classic log cake keeps hosting blissfully fuss-free.

Sweetening the season is a new whimsical log cake, Berry Mochi Delight (S$78 for 1 kg), layered with chewy mochi, blueberry compote, lemon cream cheese, pillowy light sponge and a crumble base.

Other handcrafted favourites include the hazelnut chocolate panettone (S$38 for 500 g), Christmas stollen (S$38 for 450 g) and handcrafted bonbons (S$28 for eight pieces) – all ideal for gifting or rounding out a festive table with flair.

For those who prefer dining out, Cafe Mosaic serves up a hearty Christmas buffet (S$98 to S$138 per adult) brimming with international delights and local comfort food. Expect chilled seafood on ice, flavourful laksa, roasted turkey, and tender slow-roasted black pepper beef, followed by classic log cakes and Christmas pudding. 

On Christmas Eve and Christmas Day, let the dulcet notes of live carolling and handbell performances bring a holiday sparkle and festive delight to gourmet gatherings at Carlton Hotel with loved ones.

Prices are before service charge and tax. For more information, visit: https://celebrations.carltonhotel.sg/en_SG

Mandarin Oriental Singapore

Spend an enchanting and indulgent Christmas at Mandarin Oriental Singapore, where holiday sparkle unfolds from relaxing afternoon tea to lavish festive dinners. 

At the Lobby Lounge, fall into a holiday reverie crafted in collaboration with Diptyque as you enjoy sweet and savoury teatime delights (S$88 per pax, including coffee or tea) crafted with French flavours and inspired by fig, a signature note in Diptyque’s iconic fragrances. Besides tasty bites of lobster croquettes with caviar, vanilla scones, and English fruit cake, there are creative specials of foie gras with gingerbread spice and a pistachio mousse creation with fig marmalade. Meanwhile, feast your eyes on the dazzling Diptyque-themed centrepiece that takes centre stage in the lobby.

At embu, Christmas nostalgia unfolds across abundant festive buffets (from S$228 per adult) featuring slow-cooked turkey with chestnut stuffing, pineapple-glazed bone-in gammon ham, seasonal specialities and a spectacular spread of yuletide desserts. Highlights such as the signature buche de Noel, traditional German bread Dresdner Christstollen, handmade panettone, and a medley of traditional confections evoke the comforting spirit of a classic Christmas feast.

At The Mandarin Cake Shop, winter whimsy takes the form of a trio of seasonal cakes (from S$85) to delight the taste buds. There is the citrus-bright Celadon Santa, the kirsch-kissed Forest Santa shaped like Father Christmas, and Enchanted Pinecone, a luxurious twist on tiramisu with marsala mascarpone mousse and coffee cream. 

Show your appreciation to friends, family and business associates with a festive hamper (S$528) brimming with fruit cake, pralines, cookies, and Ruinart rose champagne, and take home gourmet roasts from traditional turkey to Christmas roast goose to add joy to home celebrations. 

Prices are before service charge and tax. For more information, visit: https://www.mandarinoriental.com/en/singapore/marina-bay/festive-season

Andaz Singapore

Savour magical gourmet moments with artisanal feasts, revelry and whimsical sweet creations at Andaz Singapore.

At Alley on 25, Christmas Eve and Christmas Day buffets (S$138 to S$148 per adult) brim with handcrafted delights – from a glistening roasted turkey and maple-glazed pork ham to a whole baked salmon en croute and duck confit a l’orange.

The decadent spread also includes comforting favourites like French onion soup and butternut squash lasagna, while playful sweets such as ondeh creme brulee, a “Pineapple Tart” buche de Noel, chocolate fountains and candy bars create a merry finale. 

Weekends in December sparkle with Alley’s festive Lazy Breakfast brunch experience,  offering treats like Sarawak black pepper crab eggs benedict, truffle croque madame, eggnog panna cotta and spiced apple tartlet – perfect for slow holiday mornings.

At 665°F, celebrations take a refined turn with luxurious five-course dinners on Christmas Eve and Christmas Day (S$138 to S$148 per adult). Expect indulgent touches such as oscietra caviar, blue swimmer crab, koji-aged Angus striploin, wagyu wellington and elegant festive desserts.

Toast to spectacular skyline views at Mr Stork where packages start from S$298 per pax, featuring free-flow champagne, bespoke cocktails, delicious bites such as manchego and green olive croquettes and caramelised onion flat bread. Don’t miss the live stations dishing out New England shrimp rolls and house-smoked Atlantic salmon.

Perk up house parties with handcrafted artisanal bakes from Andaz Cake Shop including the striking Snap! Ceylon chocolate gateaux (from S$18 net), the playful Too Cubed for Xmas (S$35 net) in locally inspired flavours, and festive panettone (S$28 to S$38 net).

Prices are before service charge and tax, unless otherwise stated. For more information, visit: https://shop.andazsingapore.com/

Sheraton Towers Singapore

Celebrate a season of splendour with festive feasts, artisanal takeaways and joyful experiences from Sheraton Towers Singapore. 

The Dining Room leads the festivities with a vibrant line-up of handcrafted takeaways perfect for effortless celebrations at home. The star of the collection is the spicy turmeric coconut turkey (S$208), a Singapore-Malay twist on the classic roast, complete with tomato basmati rice, green bean kerabu and crisp mung bean crackers. 

Other bold new centrepieces include citrus tamarind-glazed semi bone-in gammon ham (S$288), barbecue-glazed beef short rib (S$198) and slow-roasted rogan josh bone-in lamb leg (S$198). No Christmas table is complete without a log cake, and the Sheraton take is an indulgent white forest creation (S$98) layered in light vanilla sponge, cream and Griottines cherries.

For convivial year-end gatherings, The Dining Room also has festive buffets (S$78 to S$138 per pax) brimming with holiday favourites such as oven-roasted Christmas turkey, barbecue-glazed wagyu rump, parmesan herb-crusted Maine lobster, fresh seafood on ice, and a joyful medley of desserts. 

Over at Lobby Bar, a charming dessert trolley afternoon tea awaits (S$68 per pax). Nibble on unlimited cakes alongside festive bites such as spiced pumpkin meringue tart, smoked salmon brioche, and the chef’s special of lobster and poached egg with crustacean veloute.

For a special Christmas, elegant Cantonese dining at Li Bai will delight with its refined five-course festive set menu (S$188 per pax), featuring double-boiled chicken soup with fish maw, wagyu with brussels sprouts, and baked seafood pumpkin rice with cheese.

Capping off the celebrations are the enchanting vocals of Victoria Chorale on Christmas Eve, performing two sessions of timeless classics.

Prices are before service charge and tax. For more information, visit: https://shopatsheratonfestive.oddle.me/en_SG

Holiday Inn Singapore Atrium

Get ready for a tropical celebration of flavours at Holiday Inn Singapore Atrium. The hotel brings sunshine to festive takeaways with a vibrant Christmas spread starring a new tropical Thai turkey (S$178 net for 4 kg) marinated in pineapple XO sauce and served with Asian achar and golden egg fried rice.

Rounding out a crowd-pleasing line-up are slow-roasted beef short rib (S$178 net for 1.5 kg) and char siew rack of lamb (S$188 net for 1 kg), honey-glazed gammon ham (S$168 net for 2.8 kg) and 13-spice roast chicken (S$78 net for 1.8 kg).

Sweet indulgence comes in the form of two new log cakes: the Golden Tropic Noel Log Cake (S$98 net for 1 kg), made of coconut mousse and mango lemongrass confit, and the decadent Enchanted Forest Chocolate Log Cake (S$98 net for 1 kg), which oozes with rich dark chocolate butter cream, cremeux and Valrhona pearls.

For fuss-free parties, the hotel’s festive bundles ensure abundance at the tables. Santa’s Summer Bundle (S$358 net, serves 10 to 12) comes with roast turkey, honey ham, chocolate log cake and wine, while the Tropical Thai Bundle (S$318 net, serves eight to 10) includes the Thai turkey, pan-seared salmon, log cake and wine.

Enjoy a halal-certified, one-for-one Christmas buffet (from S$59 per pax after discount) at the Atrium Restaurant, where a generous spread of seafood, Christmas roasts and Asian signatures await. Highlights include cajun turkey shawarma, chicken cordon bleu, beef cheek Provencal, chilli crab with mantou, and a carving station serving a rotation of prime beef ribs, roast turkey and chicken honey-baked ham.

On Christmas Eve, the feast includes premium treats such as flame-roasted bamboo lobster, snow crab legs and white chocolate fondue to elevate the celebration.

Up to 20 per cent discount available. For more information, visit: singaporeatrium.holidayinn.com

Raffles Sentosa

Have yourself an enchanting Christmas at Raffles Sentosa Singapore, where beautifully curated dining takes place among tranquil gardens and private pools in Singapore’s first and only all-villa resort. 

At Empire Grill, chef Bjoern Alexander presents modern Tuscan menus kissed with smoky aromas from the Josper grill. Christmas lunch and dinner (from S$208 per pax) showcase dishes like smoky sweet onion broth, red prawn ravioli, charcoal-grilled duck breast or grilled lobster, and a luscious chocolate ganache dessert. 

For celebratory holiday gatherings, Royal China adds Cantonese flair to Christmas feasting with festive menus (S$188 per pax with a two-pax minimum) by executive Chinese chef Ling Heng Yao and his team. Expect elegant East-meets-West highlights from roasted suckling pig and traditional duck to pan-fried turkey “guo tie” and indulgent A5 Miyazaki wagyu with foie gras. 

Festive cheer continues at the charming Chairman’s Room, the perfect place to unwind with classic negronis reimagined with wintry flavours. From pine needle honey in the Evergreen Whisper to cranberry-ginger liqueur in the Crimson Berry (S$25 per cocktail), every sip conjures up yuletide memories. 

Guests can also recreate the Raffles Christmas magic at home with festive takeaways. Make an impression at the table with Raffles turkey (S$208 net), which comes with sides like charcoal grilled potatoes and grilled onions. 

For a contemporary touch, add the barbecue ham with special curry leaf grill sauce (S$198 net) and finish off with a sweet surprise – an artisanal panettone in limoncello cream and lemon peel or pear and chocolate (S$68 each). 

Prices are before service charge and tax, unless otherwise stated. For more information, visit: https://www.raffles.com/sentosa/whats-on/raffles-festive-2025/

The Fullerton Hotels

Revel in nostalgic flavours, elegant celebrations and lavish feasts at The Fullerton Hotel Singapore and The Fullerton Bay Hotel Singapore, where year-end parties are set against Singapore’s most spectacular skyline.

Across both hotels, festive dining spans several venues. Town Restaurant (from S$68 per adult) serves an international and local spread with carved roasts and charcoal grilled specials in an al fresco setting. La Brasserie (from S$48 per pax) trots out set meals and decadent brunches with seafood, roasts and desserts. Elegant afternoon teas await at The Courtyard and The Landing Point (from S$68 per pax), while Christmas Day brunches at Town Restaurant and Jade (from S$278 per adult) are accompanied by live music, free-flow options and family-friendly touches such as Santa appearances and kids’ playrooms. 

For cosy home celebrations, the Festive Takeaway Collection shines with gourmet roasts and artisanal sweets. This year’s new stunners include the braised Five Founders beef hindquarter (S$198 net), and the roast turkey percik (S$258 net), inspired by Malay ayam percik and served with tomato-infused rice, sambal ikan bilis, and tempeh. 

For effortless hosting, the Festive Brunch at Home set (S$568 net) feeds four to six with over a dozen dishes, from poached seafood to premium beef and turkey roulade.

The dessert line-up dazzles with creations like the limited-edition The Fullerton Cassis Mont Blanc (S$128 net) and the back-by-popular-demand pandan coconut snow yule log (S$79 net). Don’t forget to pop festive bakes into the cart, like the black forest panettone (S$68 net) and classic rum fruitcake (S$42 net). Gift seekers can delight in the Festive Greetings Gift Box (S$168 net) or the luxe Festive Elegance Gift Hamper (S$338 net).

Prices are before service charge and tax, unless otherwise stated. For more information, visit: https://shop.fullertonhotels.com/

The Capitol Kempinski Hotel Singapore

This festive season, The Capitol Kempinski Hotel Singapore sets the stage for merry feasting with set menus, lavish buffets and gourmet takeaways perfect for joyful gatherings. 

At 15 Stamford Restaurant, indulge in refined three-course set lunches (S$45) featuring starters such as tiger prawns with beetroot or a crisp heirloom tomato salad, and hearty mains of Greenland halibut, turkey roulade or roasted maitake. Three-course and four-course set dinners (from S$78) feature sumptuous mains such as John Dory with XO butter, turkey and duck, and Bangalow pork belly. Finish the meal with sweet endings such as the warm orange ginger trifle or rich salted caramel semifreddo.

The celebrations continue with lavish Christmas buffets. Expect overflowing seafood, beautiful roasts including Angus beef wellington and maple sherry glazed gammon ham, plus pass-around treats including waffles with fried chicken and pan-seared foie gras. End on a sweet note with classics such as log cake, panettone, and stollen.

The hotel’s semi-alfresco Capitol Bistro.Bar. Patisserie offers its own festive charm with three-course lunches (S$38) and dinners (S$58). Tuck into comforting soups, Fjord trout, braised Angus beef cheek, turkey with roasted potatoes and macerated apples and elegant desserts. Semi-buffets (S$88 per adult) on Christmas Eve and Christmas Day comes with a choice of mains such as Chilean sea bass or wagyu beef tenderloin.

For homely celebrations, Kempinski’s festive takeaways include spice-rubbed whole turkey, Angus beef cote de boeuf, and new barbecued pork ribs. Sweeten the season with a rum fruit cake, stollen and a raspberry pistachio kunafa and raspberry yule log, or gift a luxurious festive hamper (from S$288 net).

Prices are before service charge and tax, unless otherwise stated. For more information, visit: https://www.kempinski.com/en/singapore

Fairmont Singapore

Round up your year in a heartwarming glow as Fairmont Singapore’s generous takeaway collections bring effortless at-home celebrations with hearty roasts, Italian classics from Prego and elegantly crafted desserts.

The Soiree Christmas Bundle (S$248, serves five) is a delicious delight with its wood-fire roasted poulet, cranberry and orange honey-glazed ham, and side dishes of charred truffle brussels sprouts with glazed chestnut and bacon, herb-roasted potatoes, and caramelised onions. Sweet endings include a traditional brandy rum fruit cake and red currant cream cheesecake.

The Natale con Prego set (S$358, serves five) created by Prego’s head chef Davide Bizzarri channels Italian warmth with Puglia burrata, charred Fremantle octopus, in-house cured pork meatballs and Prego’s signature seafood lasagna with saffron bechamel. The feast continues with roasted cranberry orange ham and red wine slow-braised wagyu beef cheeks, finishing with two Prego favourites: the grand cru chocolate yule log and red currant cream cheesecake.

Perfect for larger gatherings, the Traditional Christmas Bundle (S$688, serves five) impresses with a slow-roasted tom turkey with bacon chestnut stuffing, accompanied by cranberry and orange-glazed ham, and barramundi fillet en papillote. 

The generous sides range from red wine poached pears and butternut squash salad to veal sausage casserole and truffle brussels sprouts. Land on a sweet high with the grand cru chocolate yule log and Christmas stollen.

Hosts may also build their own celebration with a la carte roasts such as foie gras turkey roulade, wagyu striploin, and glazed bone-in gammon ham, plus handcrafted sweets including an elegant Mont Blanc, Swiss chocolate moelleux and festive confectionery perfect for gifting.

Prices are before service charge and tax. For more information, visit: https://fairmontathome.com/

data.title: India central bank cuts repo rate by 25 basis points, boosts liquidity
data.description:
[MUMBAI] The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) cut its key repo rate by 25 basis points on Friday (Dec 5), in line with a consensus view, as record low retail inflation and a benign outlook for prices provided ample room to further support economic growth.

The RBI also decided to conduct open market operations of one trillion rupees (S$14.4 billion) and US$5 billion in forex swaps to add liquidity and speed up transmission of lower rates.

The six-member monetary policy committee (MPC) voted unanimously to lower the repo rate to 5.25 per cent and has now cut rates by a total of 125 basis points since February 2025. It held rates in August and October.

The Indian economy is facing a “rare goldilocks” period, RBI governor Sanjay Malhotra said.

Since October, the Indian economy has seen rapid disinflation leading to a breach of the central bank’s lower threshold of tolerance, said Malhotra, adding that growth had remained strong.

A majority of economists in a Reuters poll conducted ahead of last week’s GDP data had expected the repo rate to be reduced by a quarter point at the policy meeting, followed by a pause till 2026.

However, some analysts and market participants had pared down their rate cut bets after data showed the South Asian economy expanded at a sharper-than-expected clip of 8.2 per cent in the July to September quarter.

On the other hand, retail inflation stood at an all-time low of 0.25 per cent in October and is expected to remain soft in the coming months.

The MPC retained the monetary policy stance at ‘neutral’. REUTERS

data.title: What obesity and palliative care reveal about the economies of health and healthcare
data.description:
AT FIRST glance, the two fields of obesity and palliative care seem worlds apart. One is associated with lifestyle and excess, the other with decline and end-of-life.

Yet, to a health economist, they share surprising similarities that reveal how society values – and often misprices – health and healthcare.

Neither obesity nor palliative care is a well-defined concept. Obesity is often measured based on body mass index (BMI) – a ratio of weight to height, with specific cut-off points for what defines normal weight, overweight and obese, which interestingly vary by race but not gender. Although reasonable as a screening tool, BMI is an imperfect measure that does not distinguish between fat and muscle mass.

data.title: The making of a very special whisky and an exclusive peek inside its ageing cellar
data.description:
WHAT WOULD IT BE LIKE to deliver the full and rich flavours of a whisky that’s normally sniffed and tasted only by blenders? Forty years ago, Nikka Whisky sought to answer that question when it developed an expression named From The Barrel (FTB).

The result is a blend of over 100 different batches of the Japanese company’s whiskies, including malt whisky from its Yoichi distillery and grain whisky from its Miyagikyo distillery. The formula also undergoes a three-month “marriage” process – filled into oak puncheon casks to round off its edges and harmonise its various components.

Full-bodied and powerful, the high-strength (51.4 per cent ABV) spirit – with floral and fruity notes and flavours of toffee, caramel, vanilla, oak, ginger and brown sugar that draw to a long, warm and spicy finish – went on to be well-loved around the world, garnering several awards along the way.

Even FTB’s now-iconic clear glass bottle – square and squat, topped with an aluminium screw cap and finished with a simple, white paper label – is a physical representation of its robustness as “a small block of whisky”.

To mark the blend’s 40th anniversary, Nikka released the limited-edition FTB Extra Marriage – which, as its name suggests, has undergone an additional three-month marriage process. The result is further harmonisation; while this reduces some of the original’s sharpness, it gives the whisky a more refined and richer flavour.

To fully appreciate FTB and explore this elevated special edition, we go behind the scenes in the producer’s Miyagikyo distillery and – in a first for foreign media – access its largest barrel storage site in Tochigi.

Miyagikyo distillery

Nestled in a quiet valley surrounded by forests and streams in Sendai, north-eastern Japan, the Miyagikyo distillery site was approved by Nikka founder Masataka Taketsuru (1894-1979) for the river’s pure, high-quality water. 

This is notable, given that Taketsuru is known as the father of Japanese whisky, having come from a sake-brewing family and, from 1919, learnt whisky production in Scotland’s distilleries.

Miyagikyo is Nikka’s second distillery, and its malt whiskies have a different character from those of Yoichi, its first – located in Hokkaido for the cool climate similar to the Scottish Highlands’. 

Miyagikyo is also a fulfilment of Taketsuru’s dream of the ideal blended whisky: It is here that Nikka’s grain whiskies are made using traditional Coffey stills – invented in 1830 by Aeneas Coffey to enable continuous distillation.

At the distillery, visitors can join a guided tour to check out the production facilities and participate in a blending experience. Not only did we see pot stills up close, we also got to lay our eyes on the Coffey still imported in 1963 from its now-defunct Scottish manufacturer, Blairs.

The still is marked by decades of use, made all the more obvious by the gleaming, new, Japan-made version standing beside it. It has since been decommissioned, though Nikka continues to operate its other original Coffey still, imported from Scotland in 1966.

At the warehouses, different cask types filled with new-make malt whiskies rest patiently as their precious contents mature. At the cooperage, we were treated to the dramatic sight of casks being charred – the char layer coaxes more flavours out of the whiskies.

The blending class is the highlight. Visitors get to nose and taste five Nikka whiskies and practise blending them before creating their personal favourites to be bottled, labelled and brought home.

Tochigi plant

In Tochigi prefecture, 100 km north of Tokyo, one experiences the relaxed vibes of rural Japan. Picked precisely for its rich, natural environment that’s ideal for maturation, this bucolic setting is home to Nikka’s main storage site – where grain whiskies are aged and blended whiskies undergo re-casking.

The plant in Saotome, Sakura City, sprawls 237,000 square metres (equivalent to five Tokyo Domes). Not open to the public, the 13 buildings can accommodate a total of more than 200,000 barrels.

And how many people run the entire operation? Just 38. In other words, jobseekers unaccustomed to working on a very precise schedule need not apply.

But if you’re on-staff, your workplace is picturesque – lush, forested mountains are the backdrop to Nikka’s brick-coloured buildings and whisky barrel mounds.

Our exclusive media access allowed us to explore the producer’s high- and mid-rise warehouses, re-casking facility and cooperage.

Stepping into one of its ageing cellars, where rows of casks are stacked neatly from floor to ceiling, we are hit by the intensity of the evaporating whisky. It is an intoxicating and aromatic experience, what with the angel’s share mixing with the scent of wood in the moist environment.

40 years of the golden liquid

Having visited Nikka’s distillery and ageing cellars, we develop a greater appreciation for the craft that goes into creating FTB. While the special-edition FTB Extra Marriage has a deeper and richer profile, its bottle design retains the minimalistic vocabulary of the original.

But instead of a paper label, Nikka has opted for a classy silver version printed directly on the glass, the words etched in negative relief.

At the new expression’s unveiling in Tokyo, Naoto Ono, president and chief executive officer of the Asahi-owned Nikka, noted that the group’s spirits business is “tiny” with big potential for growth. “We have the opportunity to produce more, and can be one of the greatest strengths (in the) portfolio.”

FTB Extra Marriage comes as a lone 500 ml bottle (limited to 52,000), or in a three-bottle set (just 5,000 available) with Malt Pieces of the Barrel and Grain Pieces of the Barrel. The assortment is meant to let customers try to “decode” FTB’s formula by learning from the different flavour profiles.

In any event – extra marriage or not – the whisky was always meant to be enjoyed, rather than collected. And we surely can toast to that.

data.title: Warner Bros begins exclusive deal talks with Netflix
data.description:
[LOS ANGELES] Warner Bros Discovery has entered exclusive negotiations to sell its film and TV studios and HBO Max streaming service to Netflix, according to sources familiar with the discussions.

Netflix is offering a US$5 billion breakup fee if regulators do not approve the deal, said the sources, who asked to not be identified because the discussions are private. The two companies could announce a deal as soon as in the coming days, assuming talks do not fall apart, the sources said. The move suggests Netflix has pulled ahead of Paramount Skydance and Comcast, who were also competing for the asset.

Prior to the closing of the sale, Warner Bros – valued at more than US$60 billion overall – will complete the planned spinoff of cable channels including CNN, TBS and TNT.

A deal, if consummated, would bring seismic change to the entertainment industry, joining the world’s dominant paid streaming service with one of Hollywood’s oldest and most revered studios.

The acquisition marks a dramatic strategic shift for Netflix, which has never done a deal of this scope. The streaming pioneer grew to become Hollywood’s most valuable company, without the benefit of a content library or studio, by licensing programmes from others and then expanding into original content. Representatives for Warner and Netflix did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

With the purchase, Netflix becomes the owner of the HBO network, along with its library of hit shows such as The Sopranos and The White Lotus. Warner Bros assets also include its sprawling studios in Burbank, California, along with a vast film and TV archive that includes Harry Potter and Friends.

It would represent a setback for Paramount Skydance chief executive officer David Ellison, who kicked off the bidding for Warner Bros with multiple unsolicited bids.

Warner Bros formally put itself up for sale in October after receiving interest from several parties. Comcast also made offers.

The bidding got contentious, with Paramount accusing Warner Bros of operating an unfair process that favoured Netflix. A Dec 3 letter from litigation counsel representing Paramount called the sale process “tainted” and suggested Warner Bros had titled the auction in Netflix’s favour.

Paramount argued in a Dec 1 letter to Warner Bros that its bid was more likely to pass muster with regulators around the world.

The traditional TV business is in the midst of a major contraction as viewers shift to streaming, the world that Netflix dominates. In the most recent quarter, Warner Bros cable TV networks division reported a 23 per cent decline in revenue, as customers cancelled their subscriptions and advertisers moved elsewhere.

Founded almost three decades ago as a DVD rental company sending discs to customers by mail, Netflix finished 2024 with US$39 billion in revenue. Its market value is roughly US$437 billion. Warner Bros, founded in the 1920s, had more than US$39 billion in sales.

Warner Bros’ iconic content would give Netflix powerful programming to sustain its lead over challengers such as Walt Disney and Paramount. The deal will certainly face antitrust scrutiny in the US and Europe, and has already raised some red flags.

California Republican Darrell Issa wrote a note to US regulators objecting to any potential Netflix deal, saying it could result in harm to consumers. Netflix has argued that one of its biggest competitors, however, is Alphabet’s YouTube. Utah Senator Mike Lee, also a Republican, echoed Issa’s concerns this week.

Netflix’s interest in Warner Bros also sent shivers through Hollywood. The company has largely refused to release films in theatres, occasionally giving its original movies limited runs in cinemas. BLOOMBERG

data.title: Singapore retail sales growth picks up to 4.5% in October
data.description:
[SINGAPORE] Singapore retail sales grew 4.5 per cent year on year in October, accelerating from the previous month, with most industries recording higher sales, data from the Singapore Department of Statistics showed on Friday (Dec 5).

Retail sales in September were revised down to 2.7 per cent year on year.

Excluding motor vehicles, retail sales grew 3.7 per cent in October, picking up from September’s revised 1.8 per cent expansion.

On a seasonally adjusted month-on-month basis, retail sales rose 2.3 per cent in October, compared with a 1.7 per cent decline in the previous month.

Excluding motor vehicles, retail sales expanded 2.9 per cent on the month, against September’s 2.6 per cent contraction.

Retail sales growth

Year on year: 4.5 per cent

Year on year, excluding motor vehicles: 3.7 per cent

Month on month, seasonally adjusted: 2.3 per cent

Month on month, seasonally adjusted, excluding motor vehicles: 2.9 per cent

Total retail sales value: S$4.4 billion

Year on year, sales grew in 10 of the 14 categories:

  • Department stores: 2.8 per cent
  • Supermarkets and hypermarkets: 3.6 per cent
  • Mini-marts and convenience stores: 3.3 per cent
  • Motor vehicles: 9.7 per cent
  • Cosmetics, toiletries and medical goods: 6.9 per cent
  • Furniture and household equipment: 2.9 per cent
  • Recreational goods: 20.4 per cent
  • Watches and jewellery: 25 per cent
  • Computer and telecommunications equipment: 3.1 per cent
  • Optical goods and books: 6.9 per cent

Other categories posted a decline:

  • Food and alcohol: -2.5 per cent
  • Petrol service stations: -17.4 per cent
  • Wearing apparel and footwear: -3.7 per cent
  • Others: -9.2 per cent

Food and beverage services growth

Year on year: 2.4 per cent

Month on month, seasonally adjusted: 4.3 per cent

  • Restaurants: -0.6 per cent
  • Fast-food outlets: 4.4 per cent
  • Food caterers: 12.8 per cent
  • Cafes, food courts and other eating places: 1.3 per cent

data.title: How a lucky charm came to be
data.description:
HAVE YOU EVER WONDERED HOW Van Cleef & Arpels’ instantly recognisable four-leaf clover motif came about? Or why this symbol of luck established itself as a key icon for the French luxury jeweller?

To understand its history, it’s best to start 130 years ago.

That was when Alfred Van Cleef married Estelle Arpels in Paris. Both hailed from jewellery merchant families, with Van Cleef in 1906 partnering his brother-in-law, Charles Arpels, to open the first Van Cleef & Arpels boutique at 22 Place Vendome – where it still stands today.

Luck is a theme that the maison has woven into its creations. Jacques Arpels – Estelle’s nephew – used to say that “to be lucky, you have to believe in luck”. He also gathered four-leaf clovers in the garden of his house in Germigny-l’Eveque, presenting them to staff together with the American poem Don’t Quit as an encouragement to never give up.

Records show that four-leaf clovers – a highly unusual and thus “lucky” mutation of the common three-leaf clover – made their maison debut in the 1920s. Thereafter, the motif was joined by other symbols of fortuity, such as wooden talismans, lucky charms and benevolent fairies.

Of icons and exotic locales

Fast-forward to 1968, and out of Van Cleef & Arpels’ atelier comes a long necklace featuring 20 four-leaf clover motifs in creased gold, delicately edged with golden beads. It was named Alhambra, after the Alhambra Palace in Granada, Spain, whose distinctive quatrefoil-shaped designs resemble that of a four-leaf clover.

The name may also have something to do with the fact that members of the founding family made numerous voyages during the 1960s and 1970s; their travels to exotic locales went on to influence many of the maison’s collections and their nomenclature.

Alhambra, though, was an immediate success.

Famous personalities in the 1970s who were photographed wearing its necklaces include Princess Grace of Monaco, French singer Francoise Hardy and German-French actress Romy Schneider.

Alhambra also became widely recognised as an enduring Van Cleef & Arpels icon that’s evocative of travel and enchanting scenery. It is this powerful combination of luck in precious, physical form and the ability to conjure images of journeys across foreign lands that continues to hold sway today.

The lucky voyage continues

Every Alhambra piece pulls together the varied skills of lapidaries, jewellers, stone-setters and polishers. They bring nature’s most beautiful materials, including diamonds, mother-of-pearl and ornamental stones, to life in the four-leaf clover’s gold beaded setting.

Also applied is the traditional engraving technique of guilloche that creates delicate striations on the gold clover-leaf shapes. This pattern adds depth and dynamism to the motif, as light catches the etchings during movement.

Whether worn solo on a ring or timepiece, across various sizes on drop earrings or in glorious multiples as seen on bracelets and necklaces, Van Cleef & Arpels’ four-leaf clover is instantly distinguishable.

Over the decades, not only has Alhambra’s timeless design endured, it has welcomed new models – each an opportunity to showcase the breadth and depth of the maison’s savoir-faire.

Could luck have anything to do with the fact that fans still resonate with this pretty, four-leaf clover motif today?

We’ll take a chance and say “yes”.

data.title: China chipmaker Moore Threads soars 502% after eight billion yuan IPO
data.description:
MOORE Threads Technology, a leading Chinese artificial intelligence (AI) chipmaker, surged in its Shanghai trading debut after raising eight billion yuan (S$1.5 billion) in the year’s second-largest onshore initial public offering (IPO).

The firm’s stock soared as much as 502 per cent after being sold at 114.28 yuan a piece during the IPO. If gains hold, the debut would mark the biggest first-day pop for an IPO over US$1 billion since China’s 2019 IPO reforms, according to data compiled by Bloomberg.

The listing comes as optimism over China’s drive for tech self-sufficiency intensifies, fuelled by trade tensions and fears of US technology curbs. Moore Threads’ share frenzy stands out in an otherwise sluggish market, signalling strong investor appetite in specific sectors like this year’s AI winners.

Beijing-based Moore Threads is also among those benefiting from a market share void left by Nvidia’s forced exit. Earlier this year, regulators eased listing rules for unprofitable firms on the Nasdaq-style Star Board to bolster homegrown startups.

“A surge of this scale can be somewhat expected from the strong demand, and this is one of those flagship IPOs that will go on in history and be remembered,” said Shao Qifeng, chief investment officer at Ying An Asset Management. “However, from experience, such memorable IPOs don’t always bode well for their respective sectors as could be an indication of froth, at least in some corners.”

Proceeds from the IPO will fund next-generation projects in AI and graphics chips as well as supplement working capital. The offering ranks behind Huadian New Energy Group’s US$2.7 billion IPO in July. Investor interest in the offering was strong, with the retail portion oversubscribed 2,750 times even after a clawback, making it the second most sought-after onshore IPO over US$1 billion since 2022, Bloomberg data shows.

Moore Threads’ net loss was 724 million yuan during the first three quarters of the year, according to a Sinolink Securities note, narrowing by 19 per cent from the year ago period. Meanwhile, revenue surged by 182 per cent to 780 million yuan.

Still, its valuations remain lofty. Moore Threads’ price to sales ratio at 123 times the offer price of 114.28 yuan per share is higher than the average of 111 times for peers, according to a Dec 4 filing. The company recently asked its lead sponsor to remind investors of risks related to its valuations.

Origins

Founded in 2020 by former Nvidia executive Zhang Jianzhong, Moore Threads had started out earning revenue from graphics chips for gaming and visual rendering before pivoting to AI accelerators used in powering large language models.

A major setback came in October 2023 when the US Commerce Department added the firm to its entity list, barring access to key technologies, a move that resulted in job cuts and restructuring.

Despite the setback, investor optimism has only picked up as Beijing promoted the sector as a key part of its push into technology supremacy. The Star 50 Index, which tracks the biggest companies on the Star Board, has jumped more than 30 per cent this year, with shares of chip designer Cambricon Technologies doubling.

A successful listing could pave the way for others. MetaX Integrated Circuits Shanghai, a closely watched peer, opens subscriptions on Friday. Meanwhile, memory chipmakers Yangtze Memory Technologies and ChangXin Memory Technologies are weighing onshore IPOs that could value each at up to 300 billion yuan.

Recent listings have performed well because market sentiment has been muted, “so it makes sense for a sizeable jump at its debut”, said Chen Zunde, a fund manager at Guangdong Fund Investment. Still, some worry the IPO could syphon funds from peers, adding pressure to the market, he added. BLOOMBERG

data.title: CLI-backed SC Capital starts work on US$600 million first phase of Osaka data centre
data.description:
[SINGAPORE] Singapore-based institutional real estate investment management firm SC Capital Partners Group on Friday (Dec 5) began construction of a data centre in Osaka, which has been allocated 100 megawatts (MW) of power.

The first phase would have a total investment of around US$600 million.

Located on Nanko, a man-made island in Osaka Bay, the facility will have direct access to robust power infrastructure and major network routes. The group described this as a “prime location” for hyperscale and enterprise users.

It secured the necessary building permits ahead of the groundbreaking ceremony on Wednesday, the statement noted.

Pre-leasing discussions have started, with the project having received strong interest from leading global cloud-service providers and technology companies seeking scalable, energy-efficient capacity in the region, said the firm.

SC Zeus Data Centres, a vertically integrated data-centre platform subsidiary of SC Capital, will be the development manager and operating partner for the facility.

The project is supported by a consortium comprising an existing data-centre investment programme that SC Capital has with a wholly owned subsidiary of the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA), a major Japanese real estate developer, and SC Capital’s Real Estate Capital Asia, its flagship opportunistic fund series.

All capital required for the development has been fully secured. The project is slated to commence operations in early 2028.

SC Capital - which is 40 per cent owned by CapitaLand Investment following a S$280 million acquisition by CLI earlier this year - has called the Osaka groundbreaking a “milestone” for in its expansion into Japan’s Kansai region. This is in addition to the firm’s South Korea and other Asia-Pacific markets, where it is advancing other data-centre developments and acquisitions.

Suchad Chiaranussati, chairman and founder of SC Capital, said: “Japan continues to stand out as one of the most resilient and rapidly growing digital economies globally, underpinned by strong tenant demand, stable energy infrastructure and government initiatives.”
